speaker: interviewer
text: The name of our company is Folding Pictures, Incorporated-- myself and my partner. [INAUDIBLE] pick that name? Folding Pictures. It's a long story. OK. But we basically-- it's goofy. You sit there, and you try to come up with a company name. And it takes a lot of brainstorming just to [INAUDIBLE]. Nothing?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I asked if there was another desk that could look like the main desk, if they had rooms that had four digit numbers that maybe it was originally another-- nothing, nothing. And they keep calling this room 6013, the main desk. She goes, well, she knows you're in 6013.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead and slate.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Today is April 17, 1995. We're speaking with Mrs. Sonia Zyroff. I'm Marcia Blitstein. This is Bloomingdale, Illinois, USA. And we're conducting the interview in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let me shoot the slate again, please-- need to see that on camera for about five seconds. OK. That's fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello. OK. We'd like to begin the interview by having you state your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia Zyroff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you please spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia-- S, O, N, I, A. My last one is Z, Y, R, O, F, F.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tesler.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Spell that for us, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: T, E, S, L, E, R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever use any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 27, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Figure it out. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to forget counting.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. You can't be in the line of sight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can't what?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can't be in her line of sight. Because she's gonna talk to you two just naturally. So why don't you have a seat in the chair? [INAUDIBLE]

words.text: So why don't you have a

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a village, a suburb, of city Horochow, called Markowich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Markowich for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no problems with spelling it, because it's just the way it sounds. M, A, R, K, O, W, I, C, H.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And Horochow, if you could spell that for our records.

speaker: interviewee
text: Horochow, it was a walking distance, about 3 kilometers in walking distance. I was walking to school to Horochow. And after I got married, I lived in Horochow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell Horochow for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: H, O, R, O, C, H, O, W.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish, it was Yankel. [LAUGHS] Jacob.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, Edith. Edith.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her birth name, her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was [INAUDIBLE]. But we-- we didn't have birthday parties, exactly. So we-- I don't-- I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, you say that you were born in Markovich, but you grew up in Horochow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school, I lived in Horochow. Yeah, it was just a-- a-- a-- little suburb outside in working distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family didn't really make a move, it was just the same area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. In-- oh, we-- we didn't talk about siblings. What about your brothers, sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had four brothers and a sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your brother's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest's name was Herschel, like they call it here Harry. The second was Hyman, probably an American Howie or something, [LAUGHS] and Yosaif and Benyomin. And I was in between. And I had a younger sister, Esther. She was the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community like in Horochow? Was it was large Jewish community, small?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the city was mainly Jews. Around the area was commerce, Jewish businesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jewish community tend to be poor or wealthy, middle class? What was the situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- it's hard to compare it with American life. It's a different-- different way of living. Everybody-- I mean, the Jews didn't own any land or anything like that. Everybody had to run a little business, of mainly tailors or shoemakers or mainly professionals and people that sewing furs, making fur jackets for the winter. Mainly the trade work and the business stores were in the Jewish hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the relationships between the Jews and the non-Jews in Horochow?

speaker: interviewee
text: We really didn't-- didn't feel any-- it was not that bad. We didn't feel any-- we-- we went to schools. It was under Poland later. And, of course, mainly the Jewish kids went to schools, mainly. Some non-Jews attended, too. We really didn't feel that anti-Semitism. There was. You know, they always-- they hate the Jews, because Jesus. But personally, like, I never felt that somebody will attack me because I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family, your family members, or you personally belong to any specific Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very specific. [INAUDIBLE] show you pictures with [INAUDIBLE]. We were organized. The youth was very organized. We called Betar, which is the abbreviation of Brit Trumpeldor, who was one of the Jewish historical leaders and organized the right-- right-wing organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were permitted to even march on the streets. We had meetings. Everything was-- was legal. It was under Poland. And you spoke of-- of the Jewish community in general and-- and the kind of things they did for a living. In your family, how did your family support itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we lived outside the city, as I mentioned. So after World War I, the Jews could buy land and have it. Under the czar, they couldn't. So my parents bought some, and we lived. We had a couple acres. It was first get [INAUDIBLE] Ukrainian land was very productive. And we had, like, a-- you know, handling a farm, so horses and our own cows, and had people working on those farms. And-- so my-- my parents also had a little store, like-- mostly a grocery and also cigarettes and all these tobaccos and all of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any other languages spoken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to know all the other nationalities that we lived around. So the Ukrainians spoke Ukrainian. So we had to speak that, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you speak it amongst your family members, though? Or was that only outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you meet a Ukrainian, a non-Jew, you speak Ukrainian. If you meet a Jew, you speak Yiddish. And we also went to Hebrew schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Hebrew schools, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Right after World War I, my older brothers-- we had-- my parents had a Hebrew teacher come to the house, stay with us at home and educated the children after the-- Jews got organized after World War I to open Hebrew schools. So we had to learn all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But yet you did attend a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I graduated, sure. I went. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. I graduated. European seven grades was more than here, you graduate high school. Because from the seventh grade, you go right to college.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did go to Lwow. It was shortly before the fascism started. Just maybe a month or so, no more-- then they kicked out the Jews already. What was your home life like? Was your family very religious, or what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no question. We never knew about Reformed Judaism. We never had that. Everybody was religious, observant. Kosher was no question.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I-- I feel that the European Jew was more religious. May I say something? Certain things that bother me here-- I come around like [INAUDIBLE] speakers, some rabbis come to speak to us. And the Americans ask the questions, why that harm came on us. So they tell us the European Jews were sinners. Well, it-- it just hurts me so bitterly to hear it. As far as religion is concerned, we were more religious than-- than the average Americans. [INAUDIBLE]. I don't like when the religious community say, because the Europeans Jews were sinners.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family-- I had aunts that their husbands went to America. And they didn't want to go, because they said America, the doorknob is not treifa. They didn't want to go to America. It's non-kosher. America's not kosher. Treifa means non-kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I got the treifa part. But the part before that was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was between-- between World War I and World War II.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were more kosher. We didn't know any other way, just the way it had to be. We didn't-- we didn't-- nobody had choices of any kind. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was shabbos like in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, real traditional, real [INAUDIBLE]. We had a store, was closed. The non-Jewish people knew that they cannot come. It's a Jewish Shabbos. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that wasn't a problem closing up on-- on Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The average-- mainly, it was a farm area. And the farmers knew that on Shabbos you don't go to the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- do you remember any particular literature in your house, as a child or-- or as a teenager? What things were read in your family? What magazines? What newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Newspapers-- I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's nothing in particularly. We had, in our home, religious symbols hanging on the wall. My-- my mother was quite Zionistic, grew up very much so from a different city. So she had pictures of Theodor Herzl. And she had a picture called a mizrah. Mizrah means the east. And it was all the Jewish symbols on that picture. It was very traditional for Jews to have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Nazis first walked in, in June '41-- they surprised us during the night. We'll talk about it later. But when they came to our house and they saw that mizrah on the wall, they knew right away that we were Jewish and took us all out to be killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the first-- it was not the Nazi group. It was the regular soldiers. It was about six of them. They were going from house to house to-- to find Russian soldiers. Because we were occupied by Russia in 1939. And they took us all out to be killed. And I had an infant baby, and I was holding. My husband was drafted. So one of them looked at the baby and said, leave them for a while. We'll kill them later. And they walked away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how aware was your family in the Ukraine? How aware were they of Nazism, of-- of the changes that were happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we knew that they didn't like Jews. We knew. But somehow-- I don't know. They had non-Jewish friends. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was not like they would kill somebody or beating up or-- we really didn't suffer much of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents had no plans to leave the country? They never thought of it? They never thought of it that bad? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me tell you. My father's family went all to America. My grandma died in St. Paul. That's after First War I. And my father had two brothers. They went to the United States. One in St. Paul is living, and one in Boston. My father had a sister. She was living in Montreal. My father was the only one. He did not want to go to the States. Because his older brother was more educated than my father, was a [INAUDIBLE]. In fact, he became a [NON-ENGLISH] in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wrote a letter to my father saying that he's working in a factory. So my father said, I'm not going to go in America to work in factory if my brother, Abraham, [INAUDIBLE], can work in the factories, that kind of educated. So we had our own home and the land. And we were comfortable. Were there, at any point, anti-Jewish actions in your community that-- that made you start to realize what was about to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, the-- oh, sure. We felt it. We knew what went down, what's going on. And oh, just-- sure, there were-- there were-- after World War I, some of them during World War I, a lot of Ukrainians went out of there. Because the war settled there. They went to deep Russia. After they came back after the war to their homes, then they finally started picking a little more on the Jews. And-- and the matter of fact that one family-- my parents still [INAUDIBLE] while they were rebuilding their home that was damaged during the war. Their sons captured my mother and father during the hiding as I'll tell you later. They were hiding 15 months. And then they walked out one night to ask for bread. And somebody told on them, and he sat on a horse. And it was at the winter time. My mother and father were running barefooted on the snow. And according to the foot marks in the snow, the-- that guy who grew up in our house, a favor for nothing, captured my mother- turned very Nazi, captured my mother and father in the woods sitting under a tree barefooted and was driving them back to be killed. So they brought them to-- and after 15 months of hiding. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's get the-- the time straight. When-- at what point-- at what point did things really start to fall apart in your community?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as the Jews? When you were-- when how-- how old were you when things really started to fall apart when being a Jew became a terrible problem?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what-- 1941, June 1941. See, 1939, the Russians and the Germans divided up-- divided up the country. We were in the Ukraine. That was part Poland. Near the Bug River, they made a border. And we came under the Russians. So we were there 1939 with the Russians until 1941. In 1941 in June, the Germans surprised us during the night. I think it's June 21st. I think. We went to bed with the Russians, and we woke up with German all over us. And that's when those Germans were the first-- the first group going to look for Russians. So they searched from home to home. Maybe some Russian soldiers were hiding. And this is when they broke that mizrah picture of my father and took me out to be killed. And that's when the trouble started, the big one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So members of your family, what happened to the members of your family at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In 1939, under the Russians yet, when the Russians attacked-- the-- the Germans attacked Russia in 1941. So the Russians mobilized all the young men. So during one night, they took out-- I went home from the city to live with my parents. They took my husband away during the night and two of my brothers. Two of my older brothers were married living in the city, and two single boys, and my husband. They took three out during the night. The Russians took them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my husband came back yet. He [INAUDIBLE] ran away. He came back after. But the two brothers were never seen ever since. And we were told that every Jew who went then to German prison, said they-- they right away-- you'll pardon me what I am going to say-- made them take their pants off. And as soon as they were circumcised, they were killed. So they knew. Because in our area, only the Jews did the circumcision. So we figured out that's where my two brothers were casualties. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Never heard from them or anything. So my husband came home, 1941. And then the-- the Germans-- anybody had [INAUDIBLE] all over, searching. Anybody who was in the Russian army, please come to register.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, being a Russian officer, he went to register. He was a Jew, not a Russian. But he was an officer. And they marched the Jewish war prisoners, the prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans were riding motorcycles and horses. And they made the prisoners run like that. Where they were taking them? They told us they're taking them to prisons, you know, to-- like, you know, they take to-- to prison people to-- in a war.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a special name for it. I forgot. It's a certain kind, like war prisoners. They take them in. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were driving them that fast. They had to keep up with them. One fell, one Jewish boy fell, so they gave him the bullet right away. My-- my husband, being an officer with the ambition [INAUDIBLE] diving him to the woods, he turned around and ran in the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew that nothing good is expecting him. So they gave him the bullet. That was in the beginning, the first few days, when they just marched him. So-- and they let him lay there. And they called some [INAUDIBLE] village authorities to bury him. And they buried and the woods. That was right in June '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: So for three months, I didn't know. But they told the people from the village to bury him. So the people from the village buried my husband, took out from his pocket all the papers, picture of me, picture of the baby. And they brought it. So the non-Jewish people brought it to the rabbi. And the rabbi called my parents to tell him that and my brothers. But nobody told me for a long time. So they kept the secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went with the baby one time to the-- to the German leader. Somehow, I got through everybody to the biggest leaders of the war with a question. I wanted to know in which prison my husband is going to be. So they look at me, look at the baby. And they were laughing. They said to me in German, in German, don't worry, honey, you're gonna go real soon the same way with your baby. Don't look for your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're going to do for you-- showed like that-- you're going to have it just like that. He thought I don't understand. You're going to have the same. And I went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't know that he was already killed until one time, a friend of mine comes in, a non-Jew. She looks at the baby, starts to cry in the beginning. And she said, the baby already doesn't have her father. Her father was killed. And I say, what? Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said to me, go to the rabbi. So she stayed with the baby. And I'm walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll never forget that drops of rain right on my-- I come in. And the rabbi sits down and tells me the story from beginning to the end. The rabbi's wife came in and yelling, [NON-ENGLISH]. This is the wife. Why did you tell her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to her, don't worry. I do wanna know. So my mother tried for months to bring my husband out from that grave and bring him to a Jewish cemetery. I didn't know what she was coming for. But she was all the time. But they lived in the suburb, you know. They wouldn't give her permission to bring him and bury him around. They thought it's all a joke. They're gonna bury the Jews like animals anyway. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we went right into talking about your husband. We didn't mention his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe Wydra, Moses like. Wydra was his last name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was his family from--

speaker: interviewee
text: His family was living in a bigger city, far away. Nobody left alive from his family. I looked after the war, didn't find them. They were from Kovel, yeah, a city Kovel. It's all in the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us about your baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him in a kibbutz, by the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in a kibbutz getting ready. Now, this I have to put in. So I went to kibbutz, because I belonged to the Zionist organization. And you had to work in that kibbutz a certain length of time before you get some papers to immigrate to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came to that kibbutz. When I met my husband, he was already there a long time. And he already deserved to get the legal-- it's-- it was called a-- a visa of some kind, a special name for it-- a paper that he can immigrate to Israel. But he got to go to Warsaw, Poland, first and arrange the papers. So we decided-- he decided to take me along on his papers. So I didn't have to stay in the kibbutz that length of time in order to be eligible to go. So we traveled on a truck, military-- but different ways-- several days. And we got into-- to Warsaw, Poland. And then we came to the big meeting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, they called us to the meeting. And they said to us, you have to go back. Our Warsaw, Poland, and Lodz, Poland, was jam packed with Russian-- with German youth. There were all running away from Germany. They're sleeping on floors in synagogues and in other Jewish public places. So they are entitled to get out and go to Israel. And you go home and wait. We had no choice. We went home. They took another couple from Germany, a couple-- just a girl and a boy, and married them-- fiction marriage, that two can go on the same certificate on our place. They took off for Israel, and we had to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] So after you found out that your husband had been killed, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what-- what happened in your life then? You had the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your baby. Tell us what you did then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I stayed for a while in-- in the city where I lived in, my apartment. And after a while, I went back to my parents with the baby. I didn't want to be alone in the city. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there, at any point, a ghetto set up in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Horochow was a big ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When was that set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's set-- 1941, the Germans marched in. And the first thing they did it, first month, the Ukrainians demanded from the German government to give them the SS group. And one month later, they came from house-- that was 1941-- from house to house, where it was a young Jewish man or middle aged man, take him out. And they told us they'd taken him to labor. And we hoped that it was labor. They were bringing us false letters, the Ukrainians, with lies in order to take money, whatever. They-- they took all the young people out as I found out when I came back. The mayor told me that there was another grave of these all the young men. And in the tape, you'll hear the mayor telling me in Russian language-- and I interpret it for my son into English-- that the mayor says that 350 to 400 young men are in a separate grave. Just soon as they took them out of their homes, they brought them there. And they locked them up in-- in special places and fed them with salt, herring only, and no water to drink. They thought they will die that way. They didn't die that fast. Then they killed them all. There was another grave, except the one in the picture that I have of 4,000. This is another of the young men, the same area, in the same wooded area.
